research.topic: flashgrep v0.2.7 (wgqqqqq/flashgrep) — architecture deep-dive and OmniWeave integration assessment
key_findings: flashgrep is a PRIVATE Rust binary (wgqqqqq/flashgrep, v0.2.7, published 2026-05-12). GitHub API returns 404 — the repository is not public. BitFun bundles 6 prebuilt musl/MSVC/Darwin binaries (~7-9 MB each, statically linked: only links libobjc/CoreFoundation/IOKit/libiconv/libSystem on macOS). There is no source code available for public inspection. LICENSE is unknown — OmniWeave CANNOT legally wrap or redistribute this binary without explicit permission.

flashgrep's data structure is a SPARSE N-GRAM inverted index (default tokenizer='sparse-ngram', not trigram). The binary exposes `--tokenizer [trigram | sparse-ngram]` at build time. SparseNgramTokenizer collects both document and query token hashes. This aligns with Cursor's published sparse n-gram architecture (CRC32-weighted variable-length n-grams, min 3 chars / max 8 chars by default), NOT classic fixed-length 3-gram (trigram) indexing.

The index architecture is a SEGMENTED INVERTED INDEX with a Lucene-style tiered merge policy: modules flashgrep::index_engine::{segment, manifest, merge, reader, writer, delete, gc, workspace} are all present in the binary. The index has two physical storage layers: a base_snapshot (immutable, snapshotted) and a workspace_overlay (mutable, live edits). The overlay auto-checkpoints every 1,024 ops (configurable) and merges after 2s/10s delays.

The DAEMON PROTOCOL is JSON-RPC 2.0 over stdio (or TCP port 4597). Supported methods: initialize, initialized, ping, base_snapshot/build, base_snapshot/rebuild, task/status, open_repo, get_repo_status, search, glob, close_repo, shutdown. Protocol has progress/status_notifications/task_notifications capabilities. BitFun wraps it via a Rust ManagedClient that spawns the binary with `flashgrep serve --stdio`, manages restart-on-crash, and can also tunnel over SSH for remote workspaces.

The QUERY SPEC from BitFun's protocol.rs is: pattern (regex), patterns (multi-pattern), case_insensitive, multiline, dot_matches_new_line, fixed_strings, word_regexp, line_regexp, before_context/after_context lines, top_k_tokens (default 6), max_count, global_max_results, search_mode (count_only/count_matches/materialize_matches/files_with_matches/line_matches). It is REGEX-native (powered by grep_searcher/grep_matcher crates, i.e. ripgrep's library). Glob/file-type filtering via PathScope.

Performance: 36.1x average speedup vs ripgrep on Chromium (60M lines, 4 GB tracked files). Index build time: ~79s, index size: ~2.5 GB (~58% of source). The index narrows candidates via posting list intersection, then verifies true matches with regex (grep_searcher). Uses rayon for parallel segment search. On cache-warm repeated queries the speedup is dramatic; on first-run cold queries the index build must complete first.

BitFun's fallback chain in grep_tool.rs: (1) try flashgrep indexed search, (2) fall back to shell ripgrep. flashgrep is gated by `workspace_search_feature_enabled` (config: app.ai_experience.enable_workspace_search=false by default) AND `workspace_search_daemon_available` (binary must exist). This means it is an OPT-IN accelerator, not the default path. The storage root is `.bitfun/search/flashgrep-index/{base-snapshot, workspace-overlay}` inside the repo.

CRITICAL LEGAL AND ENGINEERING GAP for OmniWeave: flashgrep is a CLOSED SOURCE private binary (wgqqqqq/flashgrep returns 404 on GitHub). OmniWeave cannot redistribute it, wrap it, or build on it without a license deal. The only documented reference to its existence is in BitFun's README and source. This is NOT the same as Zoekt (Apache-2), ripgrep (MIT/Unlicense), or xgrep (open source) — those are adoptable. flashgrep is not.

OmniWeave already has a LEXICAL SEARCH path: SQLite FTS5 with BM25 scoring over symbol names/docstrings/signatures (nodes_fts virtual table). But this is SYMBOL-level text search (name, qualified_name, docstring, signature) — it does NOT search source code bodies/content. flashgrep's value is FILE CONTENT search at scale (what ripgrep does, but indexed). OmniWeave's FTS5 is architecturally different: it answers 'where is symbol X' not 'which files contain string Y'.

The REALISTIC integration path for OmniWeave's general moat is NOT flashgrep itself, but the APPROACH: add an OPTIONAL indexed file-content search layer using an open-source equivalent. Candidates: (a) Zoekt (Go, Apache-2, trigram, proven at Google/Sourcegraph scale) — callable from Node via subprocess; (b) ripgrep as a subprocess (already in OmniWeave's PARK list for correctness parity); (c) SQLite FTS5 extended to index file bodies (token-level, not symbol-level) — stays fully in-process, no binary dep, but weaker than sparse-ngram at scale; (d) xgrep (MIT, Rust, trigram inverted index, MCP-native). None match flashgrep's closed-source integration quality, but Zoekt or FTS5-body-indexing are viable and license-safe.

research.relevance_to_general_moat: The flashgrep investigation reveals a CLEAR STRUCTURAL GAP that is real but NOT closeable via flashgrep itself. Here is the precise picture: OmniWeave (and codegraph) are pure structural graph tools — they answer 'what calls what', 'what extends what', 'where is symbol X defined'. Neither owns the 'find all files containing content pattern Y at large-repo scale' axis. flashgrep fills that gap for BitFun. The general moat question is: can OmniWeave add this axis and beat codegraph+grep for arbitrary agents on arbitrary repos? The honest answer is CONDITIONAL YES, but with hard constraints. The strategic opening is real: agent queries split into (1) structural/cross-boundary — OW wins today; (2) symbol-name search — OW FTS5 BM25 already competitive; (3) file-content regex at scale — OW has nothing, ripgrep is the fallback, and for repos >10k files it is the bottleneck that becomes visible. Adding an indexed file-content search layer (e.g., SQLite FTS5 extended to index file bodies as whole-file text, or Zoekt subprocess, or xgrep MCP) would make the `omniweave_search` tool answer BOTH 'where is symbol X' AND 'which files contain pattern Y' from a single indexed store, with structural context attached. This is the one axis where the strategic hypothesis holds: codegraph has the same gap, ripgrep is the baseline, and a unified surface (graph + indexed content search) wins on agent token cost and tool count. BUT the moat is only general if the indexed content search is zero-config (auto-builds on `omniweave init`), works on arbitrary repos (no language restriction), and is maintained. The PARK table ruling that 'cross-process×large-repo is NO-GO' applies to the STRUCTURAL edges (crossLang/invokes), NOT to lexical content indexing — those are orthogonal. File content indexing is language-agnostic and scales with repo size in OW's favor. The key risk: SQLite FTS5 on file bodies uses BM25 word tokenization which is weaker than sparse-ngram for regex patterns (e.g., 'fn auth' or 'class.*Search'). A proper sparse-ngram/trigram index (like Zoekt or xgrep) would be stronger, but adds a Rust binary dependency. SQLite FTS5 body indexing stays fully in-process and license-safe, but its recall ceiling for arbitrary regex is lower than ripgrep on a cold index, making it a supplement not a replacement. The general moat becomes: OW = structural graph + symbol FTS + OPTIONAL indexed content (FTS5 body or Zoekt subprocess). Without the content layer, OW is structurally equivalent to codegraph with a 4-edge delta. With it, OW owns the unified agent retrieval surface (structure + content + cross-boundary) that neither codegraph nor ripgrep alone provides.
risks_or_unknowns: flashgrep is CLOSED SOURCE (private GitHub repo, no public license). Any OmniWeave adoption requires a negotiated license from wgqqqqq/GCWing. This is a hard blocker — redistributing the binary or linking to it without a license is a legal risk. The binary is MIT-or-better on the BitFun side (BitFun is MIT) but flashgrep itself has no public license.

flashgrep's index size is ~58% of source code size (2.5 GB for 4 GB repo). On a developer laptop with 16 GB RAM and a small NVMe, this is fine. But for OmniWeave's typical target (mid-size repos 1k-50k files), the index overhead (index_build_time + disk) may be disproportionate if the speedup only materializes at Chromium-scale. For a 5k-file repo where ripgrep takes 0.3s, a 10s index build and 100 MB index gives no ROI.

The sparse-ngram index provides recall for LITERAL substrings and short regex patterns. It does NOT guarantee recall for arbitrary complex regex (e.g., patterns with large character classes, lookaheads, or patterns whose n-gram decomposition yields no candidates). flashgrep falls back to a scan (RgFallback or ScanFallback backends, visible in SearchBackend enum) when the index cannot narrow candidates. This means worst-case latency is ripgrep-equivalent plus index overhead.

OmniWeave's FTS5-based omniweave_search is a SYMBOL search tool (names, docstrings, signatures). Adding a file-body content search changes the tool's contract — agents expect different result shapes (symbol + source vs. file:line + snippet). The UX must clearly distinguish the two modes or the tool output becomes ambiguous and agents mis-use it.

If OmniWeave adds SQLite FTS5 body indexing (whole-file text stored in a new virtual table), the SQLite WAL file grows proportionally with corpus size. A 50k-file TypeScript repo with ~100 bytes/line average and 200 lines/file = 1 GB of source; FTS5 body index typically adds 50-100% overhead. The OmniWeave process (already holding the structural graph in WAL) would hold 2-3x more data, risking OOM on constrained CI/dev environments.

The WORKSPACE OVERLAY design (incremental updates without full rebuild) is flashgrep's key durability feature for agent use (agents write code, then immediately search their own writes). If OmniWeave adopts a simpler FTS5 body index without an overlay mechanism, freshness degrades: after an agent edits a file, the body index is stale until the next re-index cycle (OW's current 2s debounce watcher). For fast agent edit-search loops this is acceptable; for CI or batch indexing it is fine.

Zoekt (the open-source alternative) is written in Go, and OmniWeave is TypeScript/Node. Calling Zoekt as a subprocess adds a Go runtime dependency and binary bundling complexity equivalent to flashgrep — same engineering cost, same distribution problem, different license. Apache-2.0 vs unknown is a real advantage for Zoekt, but the integration architecture is identical: spawn daemon, JSON protocol, manage lifecycle.

research.topic: BitFun agent-context-economy ideas transferable to OmniWeave
key_findings: IDEA 1 — KV-cache-stable output ordering (REAL lever, BUILDABLE). BitFun's cache-friendly-message-structure.md documents a strict, fixed injection order: system-prompt → tool-definitions → collapsed-tool-listing → skill-listing → agent-listing → user-context → conversation-history. The reason is simple: any reorder of the prefix invalidates provider-side KV cache. OmniWeave's MCP output lands inside 'conversation-history'; nothing inside a single tool-result affects prefix stability. BUT OmniWeave controls tool *schema* ordering and *tool listing* order in the MCP handshake. If OmniWeave's LIST response reorders tools turn-to-turn (e.g., when OMNIWEAVE_MCP_TOOLS changes which tools are visible), it needlessly invalidates the agent's prefix cache for every turn. Concrete lever: the 5-tool default surface (explore/node/search/callers/impact) should be emitted in a FIXED deterministic order in every MCP initialize response, and tool schema text should never be rebuilt unless actual capability changes. Cost: low (ordering is already stable in DEFAULT_MCP_TOOLS; risk is only in dynamic tool-set paths). Verdict: REAL, TRIVIALLY BUILDABLE.

IDEA 2 — Evidence-pack / metadata-only context prefix (REAL general lever, BUILDABLE in output layer). BitFun's DeepReview evidence-pack explicitly excludes source text, full diff text, and full file contents — it only carries changed file paths, aggregate stats, domain/risk tags, packet IDs, hunk hints, contract hints, and budget counts. The purpose: a compact, stable, semantically dense prefix the orchestrator can pass to child reviewers without paying full token cost. OmniWeave's `explore` result already has a two-zone structure (candidate graph metadata + source-shown-below). The metadata zone — node names, edge kinds, file paths, line ranges, provenance tags — is analogous to an evidence pack. TRANSFERABLE IDEA: expose a dedicated 'graph-metadata-only' mode on `explore` (no source bodies, just symbol graph: node/kind/file/line/edge-kind/provenance) for agents that want to do a cheap first-pass orientation, then follow up with `omniweave_node` for bodies. This is a general lever because it lets agents front-load structural reasoning at near-zero token cost before committing to body retrieval. Cost: medium (needs a query param + formatter path). Verdict: REAL, BUILDABLE.

IDEA 3 — Snapshot-and-diff for dynamic surfaces (PARTIAL lever, NOT directly applicable). BitFun tracks skill/subagent listings as per-turn snapshots and injects only the diff when something changes, keeping the main cached prefix stable. In OmniWeave's MCP context, 'dynamic surfaces' would mean: stale-banner text and budget-tier metadata that changes as files are indexed. Currently OmniWeave regenerates the stale banner each tool call. TRANSFERABLE IDEA: if OmniWeave's stale text is stable across consecutive calls (same changed-file set), suppress re-emitting it. But this is a micro-optimization: the stale banner is ~50-100 tokens and appears inside individual tool results (conversation-history zone), not in the prefix. Provider KV-cache for the prefix is unaffected. Verdict: REAL in spirit but MARGINAL for OmniWeave's token economy — stale-banner diffing saves < 100 tokens/call and does not touch prefix cache. Low ROI.

IDEA 4 — Retrieval as KV-cache seed / fork_context for parallel subagents (REAL general lever, NEEDS CAREFUL SCOPING). BitFun's `Task` with fork_context=true copies parent session's message context + cloned prompt-cache into child, so parallel reviewer subagents share the same stable prefix and get cache hits from the parent's already-built context. The analogy for OmniWeave: when a coding agent spawns parallel sub-agents to tackle different files, each sub-agent calls omniweave_explore separately and pays full retrieval cost. If OmniWeave returned a 'cache token' representing the current graph state (an index fingerprint + query result hash), parent agents could pass it to child agents as proof they've already paid for retrieval context, and child agents could skip redundant calls. This is the retrieval-as-cache-seed idea. In practice, OmniWeave's build-fingerprint (dist/.build-id + version) already serves as a graph-staleness token — it just isn't surfaced in tool responses. TRANSFERABLE IDEA: include the build fingerprint in every explore/node response header so agents can cache-bust correctly. Cost: trivial (one line in formatter). Verdict: REAL, TRIVIALLY BUILDABLE for the fingerprint-in-output part; the full fork_context analog requires multi-agent orchestration BitFun itself owns and is out of scope for an MCP tool.

IDEA 5 — Verifiability / traceability in tool output (REAL general lever, BUILDABLE). BitFun's evidence-pack design explicitly tags every piece of evidence with source, provenance, staleness, and confidence. Its DeepReview report enrichment adds reliability signals: cache-hit, cache-miss, partial-coverage, capacity-skip, retry-guidance. OmniWeave's provenance field (tree-sitter / scip / heuristic) + confidence on crossLang edges is the same architectural decision, but it is not consistently surfaced in tool output. Currently the formatter may omit provenance on many edge types. TRANSFERABLE IDEA: make provenance and confidence first-class in every edge shown in explore/callers/impact output, not just on crossLang edges. This lets agents make correctness-calibrated decisions ('this is tree-sitter parsed, certain' vs 'this is heuristic, 0.85'). The model-output side effect: agents do fewer verification reads when they see high-confidence edges. Cost: low (formatter change, provenance is already in DB). Verdict: REAL, BUILDABLE, GENERAL.

IDEA 6 — Deep-review multi-specialist pattern (NOT a general lever for OmniWeave). BitFun's DeepReview spawns specialized read-only reviewer agents (ReviewBusinessLogic, ReviewPerformance, ReviewSecurity, ReviewArchitecture, ReviewFrontend, ReviewJudge) in parallel, orchestrated by a writable DeepReview agent. This is a product-level workflow, not a code-intelligence tool pattern. OmniWeave is a retrieval primitive, not a workflow orchestrator. Adopting a parallel reviewer pattern would require OmniWeave to become a harness/orchestration layer — which contradicts the 'fewer tools, not more' moat and the explicit NO-GO on vertical-bio/orchestration directions. Verdict: NOT applicable, opens scope tax.

IDEA 7 — Compression as 'reset once, reuse many' (CONCEPTUALLY relevant, NO build action needed). BitFun's compression flow: when context grows, rebuild a shorter stable prefix, invalidate the old prompt cache, then get many future cache hits against the smaller prefix. For OmniWeave, the analog is: a very long explore result that the agent has already processed is wasteful to re-send. The architecture difference is that OmniWeave doesn't own the conversation; the host agent does. However: OmniWeave's continuation key (`omniweave_node symbol=... file=... line=N`) is exactly this pattern — after a large explore, the agent can pin individual symbols and get tightly scoped bodies. The pattern is already present. No new build needed. Verdict: REAL but ALREADY IMPLEMENTED via nodeContinuationKey.

IDEA 8 — Configurable policy profiles / risk-tiered output (INTERESTING general idea, HIGH build cost). BitFun's SDLC harness uses configurable policy profiles (fast/assist/review/guarded/regulated) that adjust what evidence and how much detail is surfaced per task context. For OmniWeave, an analogous idea would be: expose a 'verbosity' or 'agent-context-budget' parameter on explore that adjusts tier (quick: metadata-only; normal: source+edges; deep: full blast-radius). The output-budget tiers (getExploreOutputBudget) already do this automatically by repo size. TRANSFERABLE IDEA: expose the tier as an explicit parameter so agents can override it (e.g., a high-resource agent doing architecture review opts into deep; a fast-loop agent opts into quick). Cost: medium (tool schema change + formatter). Verdict: REAL general lever, MODERATE build cost, WORTH evaluating against eval numbers.

research.relevance_to_general_moat: Three ideas are genuine general levers that improve OmniWeave for arbitrary agents on arbitrary repos (not bio/polyglot specific): (1) Provenance-first output — making provenance and confidence first-class in every edge in every tool response lets any agent calibrate verification effort against structural certainty. This is a direct agent-economy win regardless of language. (2) Graph-metadata-only mode — a cheap first-pass orientation call (symbol graph without source bodies) lets orchestrators do coarse scope assessment before paying body-retrieval tokens. This is useful for any large repo where agents want structural overview before reading code. (3) Build fingerprint in output — surfacing the graph fingerprint in every response lets multi-agent orchestrators (like BitFun's fork_context pattern) skip redundant retrieval and detect stale index state without an extra status call. All three are buildable in OmniWeave's MCP output layer (formatter.ts + tools.ts) without adding new tools or changing the tool surface. They do NOT reopen any PARK direction (no cross-process×large-repo, no orchestration, no embedding). The DeepReview multi-specialist pattern is NOT transferable — it is a workflow harness concern, not a retrieval-primitive concern, and adopting it would expand OmniWeave into an orchestrator role that contradicts its minimal-tool moat. The configurable-tier idea (Idea 8) is worth an eval gate before building: OmniWeave already auto-tiers by repo size; explicit override has unclear agent-ROI until measured.
risks_or_unknowns: RISK: Provenance-first output (Idea 5) adds tokens to every edge entry. If provenance text averages 15 chars/edge and a typical explore has 40-100 edges shown, that is +600-1500 tokens per call. Must be gated behind EXPLORE_INLINE_HARD_CEILING and measured against the agent-context budget tiers before enabling by default.

RISK: Graph-metadata-only mode (Idea 2) requires a new query parameter on omniweave_explore. Adding a parameter changes the tool schema, which per cache-friendly-message-structure insight invalidates the host agent's tool-definition prefix cache on the turn where the parameter is added. Introduce carefully — opt-in, not a default behavior change.

UNKNOWN: Whether coding agents (Claude Code, Cursor, etc.) actually call omniweave_explore multiple times in a session such that KV-cache prefix stability from deterministic tool-schema ordering matters in practice. BitFun's 98.67% cache-hit rate is measured over a full session with many turns; OmniWeave tool calls may be sparse enough that prefix reuse is marginal. Needs measurement.

RISK: The 'build fingerprint in every response' idea (Idea 4 partial) adds a fixed header to every tool result. If agents parse tool results as plain text (which most do), this is benign overhead. If a host agent like Claude Code parses tool results structurally, an unexpected header could cause confusion. Should be appended to the stale-banner mechanism already present, not a new top-level field.

UNKNOWN: Whether BitFun's evidence-pack metadata-only approach (source-text exclusion) means agents actually read less code (fewer fallback Reads) or whether agents still read the files anyway. The causal claim requires an A/B. OmniWeave's existing eval (omniweave_node replacing fallback reads) already tests this adjacent claim; the metadata-only variant is a cheaper form of the same hypothesis.

RISK: Configurable tier override (Idea 8) exposes budget internals as a user-facing contract. Once a tier param is in the tool schema, agents will depend on specific behavior at each tier level, making the getExploreOutputBudget function a public API rather than an internal heuristic. This creates maintenance burden whenever budget values are tuned.

research.topic: SOTA of code retrieval for coding agents — axes, ownership, and where OmniWeave could own a general moat
key_findings: AXIS 1 — LEXICAL-AT-SCALE (owner: ripgrep / Zoekt / flashgrep). Ripgrep is the universal baseline: zero config, universal file coverage (YAML/Dockerfile/etc.), SIMD-parallel, < 1 ms on warm cache. Zoekt (Sourcegraph) adds trigram pre-indexing for sub-50 ms on ~2 GB corpora (Android-class). flashgrep (wgqqqqq v0.2.6) goes further: persistent daemon index, ~36x speedup vs grep, ~94.6% search-time reduction, exploited by BitFun. CocoIndex-code uses AST chunking + local embeddings + SQLite for ~70% token savings on grep loops. No structural-graph tool (codegraph, OmniWeave, codebase-memory) owns this axis — they all defer to ripgrep for full-text.

AXIS 2 — STRUCTURAL / XREF (contested: codegraph vs OmniWeave vs codebase-memory-mcp vs Serena vs blarify). This is the pre-computed symbol/edge graph: definitions, calls, imports, extends, implements. Codegraph (colbymchenry, 47k+ stars) owns mindshare: 19 languages, SQLite + tree-sitter, local-first, claims 35% cost reduction + 70% fewer tool calls. codebase-memory-mcp (DeusData, C binary, 158 languages, arXiv 2603.27277) claims Linux kernel indexing in 3 min, 83% answer quality on 31 repos, 10x fewer tokens, 2.1x fewer tool calls vs file-exploration. OmniWeave's verified structural delta: 4 bridge edge kinds (crossLang/produces/consumes/invokes) + S4 dispatch graph — not present in codegraph (codegraph EdgeKind has only 12 kinds, zero crossLang/produces/consumes/invokes). OW is the only tool in the ecosystem that statically traces cross-process subprocess chains and workflow DAGs (Snakemake/Nextflow). Serena takes a different approach: live LSP wrappers (not pre-indexed graph) — compiler-accurate, enables atomic rename across 40+ languages, proven 38K→4K token reduction on cross-file renames; gap = startup latency, stateful JSON-RPC incompatible with stateless agent tool calls.

AXIS 3 — SEMANTIC / CONCEPT (owner: claude-context / CocoIndex / voyage-code / Cursor Turbopuffer). BM25 + dense vector (hybrid) is the 2026 consensus: claude-context uses BM25 + OpenAI/VoyageAI embeddings + Merkle incremental re-indexing. Cursor uses tree-sitter chunking + Turbopuffer vector store. Cody (Sourcegraph) dropped embeddings → adapted BM25F over graph, citing privacy + vector DB maintenance + poor scaling past 100K repos. CoREB benchmark finding (May 2026): short keyword queries collapse nearly every semantic model to near-zero nDCG@10 — semantic wins on 'auth flow' style queries, not exact symbol names. Amazon Science finding: 'keyword search via agentic tool use achieves > 90% of RAG-level performance without a vector database.' Codanna (Rust) combines semantic + call graph + doc RAG. This axis is overcrowded and privacy-constrained (code must leave device for cloud embeddings).

AXIS 4 — PRECISE XREF via SCIP/LSIF (owner: Sourcegraph ecosystem). SCIP (Symbolic Code Indexing Protocol, Protobuf) produces compiler-accurate definitions + references + implementations, decoupled from UI — enables cross-repo navigation, version-aware resolution. Sub-second symbol lookup at Sourcegraph scale. Requires running a per-language indexer (scip-typescript, scip-python, rust-analyzer, scip-clang, scip-ruby, etc.). OmniWeave already consumes existing .scip files (read-only, staged verify, stale-hash rejection) — it does not generate them. Blarify supports SCIP for up to 330x faster reference resolution vs LSP. Claude Code shipped native LSP (Dec 2025, v2.0.74) for 11 languages. The open gap: SCIP/LSP is per-language and requires setup — no zero-config cross-language SCIP for the long tail of languages (R, Perl, Nextflow, non-mainstream). OmniWeave's tree-sitter approach covers these without a per-language indexer.

AXIS 5 — AGENTIC NAVIGATION / ORCHESTRATION (unowned, the real open gap). CodeCompass (arXiv 2602.20048, Feb 2026, 258 trials): graph navigation achieves 99.4% ACS on hidden-dependency tasks vs 76.2% vanilla (+23.2pp), but 58% of trials with graph access made ZERO tool calls — agents don't use the tool unless explicitly prompted. BM25 achieved 100% on semantic tasks, graph won on hidden structural dependencies. The 'navigation paradox': larger context windows shift failure mode from retrieval capacity to navigational salience — structurally critical but semantically distant files are the failure mode no retrieval axis currently solves automatically. The three-tools-not-one article (zzet.org) summarizes: BM25 R@5 = 55.1% vs ripgrep 17.3% (3.2x), 96.8% R@5 on exact symbol names, but no single tool wins all query shapes. The 'shortage of policy' = orchestration layer combining lexical + structural + semantic with escalation rules and budget control is MISSING from every existing solution.

COMPETITIVE CROWDING in 2026: The structural graph MCP space is now extremely crowded. codegraph (47k stars, solo maintainer), codebase-memory-mcp (C binary, 158 languages, arXiv paper), repograph (networkx graph, SWE-bench integration), blarify (Python, SCIP support), code-graph-mcp (ast-grep, 25+ languages), Codanna (Rust, semantic+graph), understand-anything (multi-agent pipeline, 3D visualization), SDL-MCP, SocratiCode (40M+ LOC, 18 languages, 61% fewer tokens), tokensave (40+ tools, 30+ languages). Every tool claims 'fewer tokens + fewer tool calls.' The differentiating numbers are hard to verify and converging to the same benchmarks.

THE ONE OPEN GAP THAT IS STRUCTURALLY UNCROWDED: Cross-process / cross-language dependency tracing for arbitrary polyglot repos. ZERO competitors in the local snapshot have crossLang/produces/consumes/invokes edges. No competitor traces subprocess calls (Python calling R, Snakemake calling bwa/STAR, Node spawning Go binaries). This matches OmniWeave's ONLY verified structural delta vs codegraph (7v0 crossLang edges in the 2026-06-24 A/B). However, the PARK table finding holds: cross-process × large repos does NOT scale — in MAESTRO (1729 files), the benefit disappeared. The gap is real but niche (polyglot/workflow/bio repos), not general.

FLASHGREP / PERSISTENT-DAEMON-INDEXED LEXICAL is the axis NOBODY in the OW ecosystem occupies. OmniWeave has SQLite WAL + FTS5 already in place. The FTS5 full-text index is built at index time over all symbol names/bodies. But OmniWeave's FTS5 is NOT a Zoekt/flashgrep-class trigram index over raw file bytes — it indexes SYMBOLS, not arbitrary byte ranges. The genuine gap: a tool that unifies (a) symbol-level structural graph (callers/callees/impact/crossLang) AND (b) trigram/token-level full-text search over raw source bytes, served from ONE persistent daemon/index with ONE MCP surface. This is the 'unified surface' hypothesis. Neither codegraph nor any snapshot competitor does both from a single persistent index.

WHY GREP BEATS GRAPH ON SWE-BENCH (and what it implies for moat): SWE-bench repos are smaller; code has distinctive keywords; agents iterate with multiple grep calls. Graph tools win on hidden-dependency tasks (CodeCompass +23.2pp) and multi-hop cross-language chains (OW crossLang A/B). The correctness moat for structural graphs is REAL but task-conditional — it only triggers on architecturally-hidden dependencies in large, structurally complex, or polyglot repos. For small, single-language, keyword-rich repos, grep ties or wins. This is the key constraint on 'general replacement': OW can be the ONLY correct answer for a meaningful minority of tasks (hidden deps, cross-process, workflow DAGs) while tying grep for the majority.

HONEST ASSESSMENT OF OPEN GAP FOR GENERAL MOAT: The one technically viable axis that is (a) general (helps any repo), (b) not owned by any competitor in the snapshot, and (c) buildable on OW's existing TS + node:sqlite + FTS5 + tree-sitter stack: UNIFIED STRUCTURAL + LEXICAL INDEX WITH BUDGET-AWARE QUERY ROUTING. Specifically: OmniWeave already has the structural graph (symbols, edges, FTS5 over symbol names). Adding trigram/n-gram indexing over raw file byte ranges (like Zoekt's approach but in SQLite) would let a single explore call answer both 'who calls X' (graph traversal) AND 'where does string Y appear in any file' (raw-byte trigram scan), with one budget-aware router deciding which sub-index to hit. No competitor does both from one index. This is the candidate general moat axis.

research.relevance_to_general_moat:
The SOTA mapping reveals that OW becoming a GENERAL codegraph replacement requires owning at least one axis that is (a) general, (b) structurally uncrowded, and (c) not already owned by a larger/better-resourced tool. The honest verdict from this sweep:

AXIS 1 (lexical-at-scale): NOT OWNABLE by OW. flashgrep, Zoekt, ripgrep are hardened Rust/Go daemons with years of optimization. OW's FTS5 over symbol names is a subset of what they do. The gap is real (no competitor in OW's snapshot does trigram-over-raw-bytes from the same index), but building a Zoekt-class trigram engine in TypeScript/SQLite is a multi-month infrastructure project with uncertain quality versus established C/Go alternatives.

AXIS 2 (structural/xref): OVERCROWDED. codegraph, codebase-memory-mcp (C binary, 158 languages), Serena (LSP), blarify (SCIP), code-graph-mcp (ast-grep) all compete here. OW's verified delta = 4 bridge edge kinds (crossLang/produces/consumes/invokes) + S4 dispatch. This is real structural differentiation but it is niche, not general — it triggers only on polyglot/workflow/bio repos.

AXIS 3 (semantic): NOT OWNABLE without cloud dependency. Privacy constraints mean code can't leave device; local embedding models are good but require GPU/heavy deps; Cody already dropped embeddings. This is a crowded space with structural headwinds.

AXIS 4 (SCIP): NOT OWNABLE by OW. Sourcegraph controls the protocol and the indexers. OW's read-only SCIP consumption is correct positioning — use when available, not generate it.

AXIS 5 (agentic navigation/orchestration): THE REAL OPEN GAP but it requires agent-side behavior, not just a better tool. CodeCompass proved 58% zero-adoption even when the graph tool is available — the bottleneck is prompt engineering and harness integration, not the tool itself. OW's CLAUDE.md + AGENTS.md twin + hook integration is a differentiator here (zero-config adoption with pre-configured agent instructions), but this is an ecosystem play, not a technical moat.

THE HONEST GENERAL MOAT CANDIDATE: A unified structural+lexical index where the same persistent SQLite database answers both graph traversal queries (callers/impact/crossLang) AND raw-byte substring/regex queries (what grep does), with a budget-aware router in front. This would mean: (a) OW indexes file byte content into SQLite FTS5 OR trigram tables at init time alongside the symbol graph; (b) the explore tool queries both sub-indexes in one call and merges results by signal strength; (c) the agent never needs to decide 'use grep OR use graph' — one tool handles both shapes. This is the one axis where NO competitor in the snapshot combines both from a single pre-built local index. The technical risk: FTS5 trigram extension (fts5vocab) exists in SQLite but is not a full Zoekt replacement; building this would require ~2-3 months of work and careful eval. The strategic risk: if Claude Code ships native LSP + better built-in grep tooling, the gap shrinks from the agent side."
risks_or_unknowns: CROWDING RISK: The structural graph MCP space is now extremely crowded (codegraph 47k stars, codebase-memory-mcp with arXiv paper + C binary, Serena 25k stars). OW has no general star-count or distribution advantage. The market may already be saturated for pure structural graph tools.

CORRECTNESS PARITY RISK: OW's own A/B evidence (rounds 1-7) consistently shows correctness TIES vs grep on 6 question types. The 'graph wins on hidden-dependency tasks' finding (CodeCompass +23.2pp) is NOT replicated in OW's own evals — OW's eval shows correctness parity everywhere including hidden deps. This is a red flag: either OW's hidden-dependency coverage is weaker than CodeCompass, or the tasks tested don't exercise the graph's actual advantage.

CROSS-PROCESS x LARGE-REPO IS PARKED: The one axis where OW is structurally unique (crossLang edges) fails to scale to large repos. MAESTRO (1729 files) showed benefit disappearing. The general moat would need a different technical foundation than crossLang edges alone.

AGENT ADOPTION GAP: CodeCompass showed 58% zero-adoption even with explicit prompt instructions. OW's structural advantages only pay off if agents actually invoke omniweave_explore for graph queries vs defaulting to built-in Bash+grep. This is a harness/prompt-engineering problem, not a tool capability problem.

LSP COMMODITIZATION RISK: Anthropic shipped native LSP in Claude Code (Dec 2025, v2.0.74, 11 languages). If LSP becomes a first-class built-in for the major agent harnesses, Serena-style semantic navigation becomes a default, reducing the gap that OW's structural graph fills.

CODEBASE-MEMORY-MCP PERFORMANCE THREAT: DeusData's C binary (codebase-memory-mcp) claims Linux kernel (28M LOC, 75K files) indexed in 3 minutes with sub-ms queries and 158 languages — outperforming OW's WASM worker pool on raw throughput. OW's worker recycle interval at 250 files is a known performance ceiling that C binaries do not face.

FLASHGREP GENERALIZATION UNKNOWN: flashgrep (wgqqqqq v0.2.6) speedup numbers (~36x, ~94.6% reduction) come from BitFun's marketing claims, not independently verified benchmarks. The actual speedup at OW-relevant repo sizes is unknown.

UNIFIED STRUCTURAL+LEXICAL INDEX FEASIBILITY: SQLite FTS5 trigram search is possible (fts5vocab) but is not a Zoekt/flashgrep-class engine. Building production-quality raw-byte trigram search in TypeScript/node:sqlite is a 2-3 month project with real quality risk. The alternative — shell out to ripgrep from within OmniWeave's MCP serve — avoids the build cost but doesn't provide a 'unified index' story.

ZERO INDEPENDENT REPRODUCTION OF TOKEN SAVINGS CLAIMS: Every competitor (codegraph 70%, codebase-memory 10x, Serena 2-5x, SocratiCode 61%) claims large token savings. All are vendor-reported or single-author-reported on their own benchmarks. There is no independent cross-tool eval using the same tasks and repos. OW's A/B evidence (rounds 1-7) is the most methodologically rigorous in the ecosystem (fail-closed, real LLM, multiple rounds) — but it only compares OW vs grep, not OW vs codegraph vs codebase-memory vs Serena on the same tasks.

NAVIGATION PARADOX MITIGATION IS PROMPT ENGINEERING, NOT A TOOL GAP: The CodeCompass finding that checklist-at-END prompt formatting boosted G3 adoption to 100% means the 'agents don't use graph tools' problem is partially solvable by harness configuration (CLAUDE.md / AGENTS.md). This is OW's real near-term lever — not building new retrieval infrastructure, but ensuring the agent reliably invokes omniweave_explore for the right query shapes.

research.topic: Codegraph's real, general weaknesses — ranked by how buildable a win is for OmniWeave
key_findings: CONFIRMED GENERAL WEAKNESS #1 — Dynamic-dispatch synthesis gap (625 lines, 20 synthesizer functions in CG's a893156 snapshot that OW has NOT synced): Pinia/Vuex store dispatch, Redux/RTK-query, MediatR, Celery, Sidekiq, Laravel events, Spring events, C function-pointer dispatch, object-registry patterns. These are GENERAL (not bio-specific), measured as the biggest driver of CG's own effort savings (see excalidraw case in CG CLAUDE.md: broken synthesis raised reads 5–7x; closing the flow end-to-end dropped reads to 0–1). OW's callback-synthesizer.ts is 2,126 lines vs CG snapshot's 2,751 — a 625-line capability deficit on general-repo flows. OW gains the workflow/S4 functions (rS4DispatchEdges, workflowCrossLangEdges, generalCrossLangEdges) but LOSES 20 CG-only synthesizers.

CONFIRMED GENERAL WEAKNESS #2 — CG defaults to 1 MCP tool (explore-only) vs OW's 5. CG's DEFAULT_MCP_TOOLS = new Set(['explore']) in its mcp/tools.ts:683. OW's DEFAULT_MCP_TOOLS = new Set(['explore', 'node', 'search', 'callers', 'impact']) in OW src/mcp/tools.ts:937. Fewer listed tools = less agent confusion, lower salience tax. This is a real UX difference that an agent's tools/list call will expose — CG shows 1 tool by default, OW shows 5. CG's design is more aggressive on the 'fewer tools = better agent adoption' axis that both tools' benchmarks confirm.

CONFIRMED GENERAL WEAKNESS #3 — CG has a UserPromptSubmit front-load prompt hook (codegraph prompt-hook subcommand, installed via codegraph install --default yes). On structural questions it pre-injects <codegraph_context> into the prompt before the agent's first turn — raising natural-mode adoption without requiring the agent to decide to call the tool. OW has no equivalent (grep confirmed 0 hits for 'prompt-hook' in OW src/). This is architecture-level: it addresses the 'low salience / attach latency' confound that the OW benchmark itself flags as the biggest natural-mode adoption problem.

CONFIRMED GENERAL WEAKNESS #4 — CG FTS5 covers only symbol names, docstrings, signatures — not file content bodies. Both CG (schema.sql: nodes_fts over name/qualified_name/docstring/signature) and OW (identical schema confirmed) are structural-graph-only. Neither has an indexed full-text search over source code bodies. The OW benchmark Q7 (grep wins on full-text presence) is where flashgrep's indexed lexical search wins over BOTH tools. flashgrep's QuerySpec shows regex/fixed-string/case-insensitive search with a persistent indexed snapshot: IndexedSnapshot backend gives ~36x speedup vs scan on Chromium-scale repos. Neither CG nor OW plays on this axis at all — it is currently unowned territory for structural graph tools.

CONFIRMED GENERAL WEAKNESS #5 — CG has c-fnptr-synthesizer.ts (359 lines, struct-field function-pointer dispatch for C/C++) that OW has not synced. C function-pointer dispatch is the standard dynamic dispatch mechanism in C codebases (Linux kernel, CPython, SQLite). Without it, C/C++ flows break at fn-pointer boundaries the same way S4 flows broke before OW fixed them. This is a general-repo capability: ripgrep (already benchmarked), SQLite, Linux kernel etc. are all C repos where this matters.

CONFIRMED GENERAL WEAKNESS #6 — CG has worktree-awareness (src/sync/worktree.ts) that prevents a tool running in a git worktree from silently resolving the main checkout's index. The CHANGELOG reports a real bug: one user's graph went from ~1,850 to over 24,000 files due to worktree duplication. Claude Code itself creates worktrees under .claude/worktrees/. OW should verify it has equivalent protection — the research snapshot has worktree.ts but OW's sync/ needs to be confirmed as identical.

CONFIRMED GENERAL WEAKNESS #7 — The 'explore-ceiling advantage' (previously claimed as OW having 25K cap vs CG's 35-38K externalize) is INVALIDATED by reading CG's actual source: hardCeiling = Math.min(Math.round(budget.maxOutputChars * 1.5), 25000) in CG mcp/tools.ts:3324. Both cap at 25K. The stale CLAUDE.md budget table that cited 35-38K was wrong. This is an existing false claim that must be removed from any OW documentation — repeating it is directly contradicted by CG's source code and qualifies as dishonest framing.

FLASHGREP STRATEGIC ASSESSMENT — flashgrep (wgqqqqq/flashgrep v0.2.6, prebuilt Rust binary bundled by BitFun) is a persistent-daemon regex/literal-string search with an indexed snapshot backend (IndexedSnapshot, IndexedClean, IndexedWorkspaceView, RgFallback). QuerySpec confirms: regex pattern, fixed_strings, case_insensitive, word_regexp, before/after context, top_k_tokens. Measured ~94.6% search-time reduction, ~36.1x speedup on Chromium. This is a DIFFERENT axis from structural graph: flashgrep wins on 'find any line containing X across 100k files in <100ms'. OW/CG win on 'what calls X, what does X affect, how does X reach Y across language boundaries'. The two are COMPLEMENTARY, not competing — but currently unconnected. A unified surface (structural graph + flashgrep-class lexical) would beat CG (structure-only) AND grep (lexical-only, no index) on the full query space.

HONEST MOAT SUMMARY — OW's current non-evaporating structural delta over CG for GENERAL repos is: (a) 4 bridge edge kinds (crossLang/produces/consumes/invokes) in the type system — zero in CG's types.ts; (b) S4 dispatch graph (773 method nodes, 221 overrides on 4 real Bioconductor packages; CG = 0). Both are scenario-gated: bridge edges appear in 0/14 single-language repos tested. CG's non-evaporating advantages over OW's current state: 20 more synthesizer functions covering Pinia/Vuex/Redux/MediatR/Celery/Sidekiq/Laravel/Spring/C-fnptr — all general, all already validated in CG. OW's GENERAL moat vs CG requires syncing these 625 lines of synthesizer code to re-establish parity, then building forward from the bridge-edge + S4 differential.

BUILDABILITY RANKING — (1) Sync CG's 20 missing synthesizer functions: bounded copy + test, no schema change, high-confidence correctness ROI on general TS/JS/Java/Python repos. (2) Sync c-fnptr-synthesizer.ts: 359 lines, adds C/C++ dynamic dispatch. (3) Implement UserPromptSubmit prompt hook: architecture exists in CG as a template, addresses the biggest real-world adoption gap (natural mode). (4) Investigate flashgrep integration: an OW MCP tool that wraps flashgrep (if available) or SQLite FTS5 over file content bodies would fill the lexical axis that NEITHER CG nor OW currently owns — this is the only path to a GENERAL moat that beats both CG and grep simultaneously.

research.relevance_to_general_moat: Currently OW has NO general moat over CG on arbitrary single-language repos — confirmed by the benchmark (bridge edges zero in 14/14 single-language repos, correctness ties everywhere). The path to a general moat has two distinct vectors: (1) PARITY FIRST: sync the 625 lines of CG synthesizer code OW has drifted behind on (Pinia/Vuex/Redux/MediatR/Celery/Sidekiq/Laravel/Spring/C-fnptr). These are already validated by CG on general TS/JS/Java/Python repos and are additive to OW. Without this sync OW is actually WEAKER than CG on general dynamic-dispatch coverage. (2) DIFFERENTIATION: the one axis where a genuinely GENERAL moat exists that neither CG nor OW currently owns is fast lexical search at scale (flashgrep-class). Neither tool has file-content FTS; both are structural-graph-only. An OW tool that combines structural traversal (graph) with indexed lexical search (FTS5 over file bodies OR flashgrep integration) would answer a strictly larger query set than CG alone. The strategic hypothesis 'unified structural + lexical surface beats CG + grep + flashgrep for a general agent' is buildable on OW's TypeScript/node:sqlite stack — FTS5 over file content is a schema extension (add a files_content_fts table), not an architecture rewrite. The moat is GENERAL because every agent on every repo occasionally needs both structural traversal AND lexical/regex search, and today they must invoke separate tools (codegraph_explore for structure, Grep/flashgrep for text) — a single tool that answers both collapses that into one call."
risks_or_unknowns: The 20 missing CG synthesizer functions (Pinia/Vuex/Redux/MediatR etc.) were validated by CG on its own benchmark repos — but OW has NOT run eval on those repos post-sync. Syncing them could introduce edge conflicts with OW's existing workflow/S4 synthesis. Must run lang-parity.sh + eval fixtures after sync before claiming parity.

CG's DEFAULT_MCP_TOOLS = 1 (explore-only) is documented as their validated design for agent adoption — it implies CG's agent experiments showed 5 tools caused lower adoption than 1. OW's choice of 5 tools as default is not yet validated against CG's 1-tool design. This is a product decision, not a technical question, but it is currently unvalidated.

The UserPromptSubmit prompt-hook is a Claude Code-specific feature (hooks into Claude's settings.json). Its adoption impact has been measured by CG in their CLAUDE.md doc (trace-first steering ported to server-instructions + tool descriptions 'regressed wall-clock vs baseline') — injecting context pre-prompt is the mechanism that WORKS vs post-prompt steering. OW adopting this would need a measured A/B to confirm it doesn't regress for OW's specific output format.

flashgrep integration assumes the flashgrep binary is available. The binary is prebuilt for macOS/Linux/Windows (confirmed at ~/Desktop/develop/sogen/πCode/BitFun/resources/flashgrep/) but shipping a Rust binary in an npm package raises licensing/bundling complexity. An alternative using SQLite FTS5 over file content bodies is fully self-contained but would be slower than flashgrep's indexed Rust implementation — the ~36x speed gap is real for large repos.

The raison-d'etre debate (eval-results/raison-detre-2026-06-24/debate.md) flagged that the Q3 workflow effort win (16 vs 36 turns) exists ONLY on small-to-medium fixtures; the PARK table records the cross-process win evaporating at MAESTRO scale (1729 files). The claim that 'structural graph + lexical = general moat' assumes the structural side scales — this has not been tested for large repos specifically.

CG's c-fnptr-synthesizer.ts (C function-pointer dispatch) is not in OW, but the OW benchmark's C repo (sds, 75 nodes) is too small to observe the gap. The moat for C/C++ fn-pointer dispatch would only be measurable on a larger C codebase (Linux kernel, CPython). Adding the synthesizer without a C-specific eval cannot be claimed as a validated win.

The statement that CG's snapshot (a893156) includes Pinia/Vuex synthesis (confirmed: piniaStoreEdges at callback-synthesizer.ts:1944) while OW's src has 0 references — means OW's Pinia support gap is specifically that OW forked before this synthesis was added. The full Vuex dispatch synthesis (vuexDispatchEdges) is also CG-only. These are popular frameworks; the gap affects every Vue.js codebase an agent touches.

research.topic: OmniWeave vs flashgrep: scale/perf/lexical posture audit and integration architecture
key_findings: OW's FTS5 scope is SYMBOL-NAMES ONLY: nodes_fts (schema.sql line 98) indexes {id, name, qualified_name, docstring, signature} — the 'content' column is a back-reference to the nodes table row, NOT raw file content. No file body text is stored in the DB. The `files` table stores only path, content_hash, language, size, and timestamps — zero raw bytes.

Q7 (GREP-WINS) from the benchmark is the exact gap: 'Which files mention the string Rscript?' — OW returns 'partial — symbol index is not full-text'. This is a categorical limitation, not a performance one. OW cannot find arbitrary string patterns in code bodies today without reading files from disk at query time.

FTS5 trigram tokenizer IS available in node:sqlite 22.22.3 (tested live: 'CREATE VIRTUAL TABLE t USING fts5(x, tokenize=trigram)' works). A full-content trigram FTS5 table is technically feasible in-process with ZERO new dependencies. Measured expansion: 3.3x (tools.ts 220KB → 728KB FTS5 trigram index). For 200k x 5KB average files (Chromium-class): ~3GB index. Query latency for rare terms on 10k files: 0.06ms per query (vs 0.16ms for LIKE — 2.8x speedup at scale).

flashgrep is a prebuilt Rust daemon binary (7.4MB aarch64 Mach-O). Protocol: JSON-over-stdout (line-matching output: {path, line_number, line_text}). Serves via TCP (default 127.0.0.1:4597) or --stdio. API matches ripgrep's flags exactly (-e regexp, -F fixed, -i ignore-case, -g glob, -t filetype). Index expansion: 95MB for 696 docs / 35.6MB source = 2.67x. Build time: 1.4s for OmniWeave (696 docs). Query latency: 40-80ms observed (includes subprocess spawn — in-process IPC would be lower). Tokenizer: sparse-ngram (default) or trigram. Supports incremental overlay updates on file change.

OW indexing throughput ceiling: PARSE_TIMEOUT_MS=10_000, WORKER_RECYCLE_INTERVAL=250 files (WASM linear heap can't shrink), MAX_FILE_SIZE=1MiB, FILE_IO_BATCH_SIZE=10. Bottleneck is WASM tree-sitter parse per file — this is the structural-graph path, entirely orthogonal to full-text lexical search.

Three integration architectures, ranked by compatibility with OW's zero-config/node:sqlite/MCP-native design: (1) NATIVE FTS5 TRIGRAM — add a 'content_fts' virtual table to schema.sql alongside nodes_fts, populated during indexAll for files <= MAX_FILE_SIZE. Stays in omniweave.db. Zero deps. Expose as omniweave_search with pattern: param or extend omniweave_explore fallback. Storage: ~3-4x file content size. Works on Chromium-scale at 3GB index. (2) FLASHGREP SIDECAR — spawn flashgrep binary as subprocess during omniweave init/sync, query via JSON stdout. Requires bundling 5 platform binaries (~35MB total). Daemon lifecycle entangles with OW's daemon. 95MB for a 35MB repo. (3) RIPGREP SHELL FALLBACK — when FTS5 misses, exec 'rg --json' as a subprocess. Zero storage overhead, no daemon. Latency: 50-500ms per cold query on medium repos. Does NOT require an index.

The strategic gap is narrow and precise: OW wins on structure+composition queries (Q1-Q5, 5 of 7 benchmark cases). OW loses ONLY on Q7-class full-text presence queries. flashgrep/ripgrep wins Q7 because OW has no raw-content index at all. A unified surface would combine OW structural graph + full-text lexical search — letting agents use ONE MCP server for both 'find all callers of X across language boundary' AND 'find all files mentioning string Y'.

The FTS5 trigram option DOES break OW's zero-config promise at Chromium scale: a 200k-file repo would require 3-4GB of additional index storage alongside the structural graph (~50-200MB). For typical repos (< 50k files, avg 3-5KB per file), the overhead is 500MB-1GB — borderline acceptable. The PARK note on 'in-process mode NO-GO' was about keeping the MCP server light, not about FTS5 storage; those are orthogonal concerns.

flashgrep's sparse-ngram tokenizer is proprietary to that binary and not reproducible in SQLite. The FTS5 'trigram' tokenizer in SQLite is documented, stable, and gives similar functionality (arbitrary substring search, regex via LIKE fallback). The main advantage of flashgrep over SQLite FTS5 trigram is: git-commit-pinned snapshots with incremental overlay updates, multi-shard parallel search, and ~36x speedup claim at Chromium scale (200k files) — which SQLite single-file WAL cannot match at that scale.

Realistic integration verdict: For repos under 20k files (the overwhelming majority of OW users), adding a content_fts USING fts5(path UNINDEXED, content, tokenize='trigram') table to schema.sql during indexAll closes Q7 without any external binary dependency, keeps zero-config, and adds <500MB index overhead. For Chromium-class (200k+ files), flashgrep as an optional sidecar (not default) is the right path — but only AFTER proving it generalizes the moat, which requires an agent A/B on a large repo with a 'find all usages of string X' type question.
