research.relevance_to_general_moat: The general moat question hinges on one precise fact: OW's current FTS scope is SYMBOL NAMES ONLY. grep (and flashgrep) win Q7 ('find files mentioning string X') because OW has no raw-content index. This is not a performance gap — it's a categorical absence. For OW to become a GENERAL codegraph replacement, it must serve both structural graph queries (where it already wins) AND lexical/content queries (where grep currently wins). The most viable path is adding FTS5 trigram over file content in-process (zero new binary deps, tested feasible in node:sqlite 22.22.3, ~3-4x storage expansion, sub-ms latency for rare terms on repos under 50k files). This directly addresses the only provable category where OW loses today. The critical constraint: flashgrep's claimed 36x speedup at Chromium scale (200k files) requires a persistent, sharded, memory-mapped index that SQLite's single-file WAL cannot replicate at that size. For truly Chromium-scale repos, a flashgrep sidecar is technically superior — but the agent A/B evidence showing this closes a GENERAL moat (not just Q7 on one fixture) does not yet exist and must not be claimed until measured."
risks_or_unknowns: Storage overhead breaks zero-config on large repos: 3-4GB additional index for Chromium-scale (200k files). No existing eval on whether agents actually benefit from this at that scale vs just using ripgrep as fallback.

FTS5 trigram insertion throughput during indexAll: inserting full file content into SQLite alongside tree-sitter parsing may bottleneck on the main-thread SQLite write path (WAL allows concurrent reads, but writes are serialized). Not benchmarked at >10k files.

The 40-80ms flashgrep latency figure includes process spawn overhead. True in-process IPC latency (--stdio mode) is unknown from current measurements — BitFun uses Rust-side IPC, not a Node subprocess. The Node-side integration would need to manage the subprocess lifetime across OW's daemon model.

flashgrep's binary is MIT-licensed (wgqqqqq/flashgrep v0.2.6) and ~7MB per platform (5 platforms = ~35MB bundle addition). This breaks OW's current 'zero native build' design principle — the binary must be platform-matched at install time.

OW's PARK table includes 'in-process mode NO-GO' (round6: fixed layer ~34k, marginal +682 tok). This was about the MCP prompt overhead, not about full-text indexing. But adding a new tool surface (omniweave_grep or a full-text-enabled omniweave_search) may re-open the form-tax question: does the agent need yet another tool, or can it be absorbed into explore?

The 'Q7 grep wins' benchmark is on a 2-file polyglot-subprocess fixture. It has NOT been tested on real large repos where grep's throughput degrades sub-linearly with file count. flashgrep's 36x speedup claim is from their own benchmark (not reproduced here); independent validation at Chromium scale is needed before basing a moat claim on it.

Incremental update: the FTS5 trigram content table would need to be DELETE-then-reinsert on file change (same as the nodes_fts trigger pattern). At WORKER_RECYCLE_INTERVAL=250 files, this is already the sync bottleneck path. No concurrent write concern (WAL), but the write volume doubles.

The 'sparse-ngram' tokenizer in flashgrep is not open-source and cannot be reproduced in SQLite's FTS5. If a future OW FTS5 trigram table diverges significantly in recall from what flashgrep provides, the user-facing behavior differs from BitFun's benchmark claims.

strategy.core_thesis: OmniWeave's only path to GENERAL codegraph replacement is a two-vector compound move, not a single axis. Vector 1 — close the general dynamic-dispatch synthesis deficit (sync the 625-line Pinia/Vuex/Redux/MediatR/Celery/Sidekiq/Spring/C-fnptr gap from CG's callback-synthesizer.ts, plus port CG's UserPromptSubmit prompt-hook): this makes OW at least as good as CG on every common TS/JS/Java/Python/Ruby/C repo, eliminating the current state where OW is actively weaker on general single-language repos. Vector 2 — add a content_fts USING fts5(tokenize='trigram') table over raw file bytes at init time: this fills the one categorical gap both OW and CG share (neither has indexed full-text search over source code bodies), converting omniweave_search from a symbol-name-only tool into a unified structural+lexical retrieval surface. Together these make OW the ONLY local-first MCP code-graph that answers both "who calls X across language boundaries" AND "which files contain string Y" from a single persistent SQLite index with zero external binary dependencies. The moat is not correctness — it is tool-count collapse (one tool instead of graph-tool + grep) and token economy, which the research shows compounds as models weaken and repos grow.
build_plan: Phase 0 — Parity first (1-2 weeks, proves OW is not weaker than CG on general repos): Sync the 625-line callback-synthesizer deficit from CG's a893156 snapshot — port piniaStoreEdges, vuexDispatchEdges, Redux/RTK dispatch, MediatR/CQRS, Celery task-send, Sidekiq perform_async, Laravel event-dispatch, Spring @EventListener, and the c-fnptr-synthesizer.ts (359-line C function-pointer dispatch). Run harness/lang-parity.sh + eval fixture suite after each sub-batch. Gate: eval scores on TS/JS/Java/Python/Ruby/C repos must reach or exceed CG baseline. This is not a feature addition — it is debt repayment that is the prerequisite for any general moat claim.

Phase 1 — Prompt-hook adoption fix (1 week, proves OW can match CG's natural-mode adoption rate): Port CG's UserPromptSubmit hook (src/bin/codegraph.ts:1035-1093) as an omniweave equivalent. This pre-injects structural context into the agent prompt before the first turn, addressing the CodeCompass 58% zero-adoption crisis without requiring agents to decide to call omniweave_explore. Gate: agent A/B comparing natural-mode tool-call count OW-with-hook vs OW-without-hook vs CG-with-hook on three repos (one TS, one Python, one workflow). Success criterion: OW natural-mode adoption rate reaches parity with CG's hook-enabled rate.

Phase 2 — FTS5 trigram content index (2-3 weeks, proves OW is strictly a superset of grep for symbol+content queries): Add a content_fts VIRTUAL TABLE USING fts5(path UNINDEXED, content, tokenize='trigram') to schema.sql, populated during indexAll for files <= MAX_FILE_SIZE. Expose as a second mode in omniweave_search: pattern: param triggers trigram content scan; query: param keeps the existing symbol BM25 path. The explore tool's fallback path also queries content_fts when symbol seeds return empty. Gate: Q7-class benchmark (find all files containing string X) must return correct results from OW instead of 'partial — symbol index is not full-text.' Storage overhead must be measured and reported at 5k/20k/50k file tiers before merging. For repos < 50k files, overhead must be < 1 GB.

Phase 3 — Unified routing + agent-output improvements (1 week, proves the combined surface beats CG+grep on a mixed query set): Wire a budget-aware query router in explore that tries the structural graph first, falls back to content_fts trigram for queries with no symbol seeds. Add provenance-first output to every edge in callers/impact responses (tree-sitter vs heuristic, confidence where applicable — already in DB, not yet formatted). Reduce DEFAULT_MCP_TOOLS from 5 to 1 (explore only, matching CG's validated minimal-surface design), absorbing node/search/callers/impact functionality into explore's routing. Gate: full benchmark re-run (all 7 Q-types) on the same repos as the 2026-06-24 OW-vs-CG eval. OW must tie or beat CG on Q1-Q6 and WIN Q7. Zero regressions on vitest 1764 suite.

Phase 4 — Agent A/B validation (decisive experiment, see below): Run the head-to-head benchmark described in the decisive_experiment field. This is the gate that either confirms the general moat is real or kills it honestly.
strategy.why_general: The content_fts trigram index is language-agnostic — it indexes raw file bytes regardless of whether the language is R, Perl, TypeScript, Go, or YAML. Every agent on every repo occasionally needs 'which files contain pattern X' alongside 'what calls X.' Today they must invoke two separate tools (any structural graph for structure, Bash/grep for text). A single omniweave_search call that answers both collapses the tool count by one for every mixed query, on every repo type. The synthesis deficit fix (Phase 0) restores general parity on TS/JS/Java/Python/Ruby/C — the languages that cover the vast majority of real-world repos that CG's 47k-star user base actually uses. The prompt-hook (Phase 1) addresses zero-adoption on any repo type, not just bio. None of Phase 0-2 depends on the bio/polyglot/workflow scenario — they are pure general improvements. The bridge edges (crossLang/invokes/S4) continue to be the differentiator on top of this general foundation, not the foundation itself.
strategy.the_moat: The moat has three non-overlapping layers: (1) Structural completeness parity: after Phase 0, OW is the only fork that holds CG's full general dispatch synthesis (Pinia/Vuex/Redux/MediatR/Celery/Sidekiq/Spring/C-fnptr) PLUS OW's own S4 dispatch graph and workflow DAG edges — 16 EdgeKind types vs CG's 12, with no scenario where OW is weaker. CG cannot replicate crossLang/produces/consumes/invokes without a schema-level change; those are not in CG's types.ts union. (2) Unified lexical+structural index: after Phase 2, OW is the only local-first MCP graph that answers both structural traversal queries AND raw-byte substring/regex queries from the same SQLite WAL with zero external binary deps. CG's nodes_fts is identical to OW's pre-Phase-2 (symbol-names only, confirmed from both schemas). Neither CG nor any other structural graph tool in the 2026-06-24 ecosystem scan has a content_fts table — this is a categorical structural advantage, not a degree advantage. flashgrep is legally unadoptable (private repo, no public license); Zoekt adds a Go binary dependency; SQLite FTS5 trigram stays fully in-process. (3) Trust/honesty architecture: daemon build-fingerprint skew detection, provenance-tagged synthesized edges, honest empty results — these are real engineering investments with zero documented equivalent in CG's source. Not yet a measured behavioral moat (requires the head-to-head trust A/B from the PARK deciding experiments), but a structural architectural commitment that compounds over time as CG's simpler design accumulates stale-daemon incidents.
strategy.decisive_experiment: One head-to-head A/B benchmark, fail-closed, on five diverse real repos (one TS monorepo 5k+ files, one Python Django, one Java Spring, one nf-core Snakemake pipeline, one mixed C+Python): run 10 questions per repo (5 structural: callers/impact/dispatch/crossLang/workflow; 5 content: 'find all files containing string X' where X is a non-symbol literal), three arms (OW post-Phase-3, CG current, grep+Read baseline), real LLM (MiMo or DeepSeek), natural mode and forced-MCP mode, N=2 per question per arm. Gate: OW must WIN the content questions (Q7-class) decisively vs both CG and grep-on-cold-index on the 5k+ file repo (where grep's per-query cost is measurable), TIE or WIN the structural questions vs CG, and show lower total tool-call count than grep across all 10 questions per repo. A tie on content questions at this repo size means the FTS5 trigram moat is not real at meaningful scale and the strategy must revert to 'niche bio/polyglot fork.' A win on content AND structural questions across all five repos at N=2 confirms the general unified-surface moat is real. The experiment must be fail-closed (non-zero exit on auth failure/empty jsonl), results must be archived in eval-results/ with full raw jsonl and per-question GT, and must not claim correctness wins — only tool-call/turn/token economy wins.
strategy.honest_risk: At repos above 50k files (Chromium-class), SQLite FTS5 trigram adds 3-4 GB of index storage alongside the structural graph — breaking OW's zero-config promise for large monorepos. More importantly, SQLite's single-writer WAL cannot parallelize the trigram index build across shards the way flashgrep's Lucene-style segmented index does, so Q7-class query latency at 200k-file scale may still be ripgrep-equivalent (0.3-1s) rather than flashgrep's <100ms. If the decisive experiment is run on a Chromium-class repo and OW's content_fts query latency matches a cold ripgrep call, the 'unified surface beats grep' claim collapses at exactly the scale where beating grep would matter most. The moat is real and general for the overwhelming majority of repos (< 50k files, where ripgrep takes < 0.5s and the agent ROI is in tool-call count collapse, not query latency), but it may not generalize to large-monorepo scenarios — which are precisely the repos where the 36x speedup claim of flashgrep matters and where CG's simpler design (no content index, just defer to ripgrep) may actually be the correct architectural choice.
strategy.verdict: GO-with-caveats — the unified structural+lexical surface hypothesis is technically sound and buildable on OW's existing stack with no new binary dependencies, but it requires Phase 0 parity work first (without closing the 625-line synthesis deficit, OW is actively weaker than CG on general repos today and building new features on top of that deficit is the wrong order of operations).

attacks: ATTACK 1 — Phase 0 'parity first' silently reopens the maintenance-tax PARK direction. The strategy frames syncing 625 lines of CG callback-synthesizer code as 'debt repayment,' but this is the same maintenance-fork problem the raison-d'etre debate (debate.md line 19) ALREADY PARK'd: 'maintaining 8633 lines of diverged hot-path code is an indefinitely growing tax.' Syncing the 20 missing CG synthesizer functions (Pinia/Vuex/Redux/MediatR/Celery/Sidekiq/Laravel/Spring/C-fnptr) for general repos is not debt repayment — it is SCOPE EXPANSION that makes OW's differential layer larger, not smaller. The correct framing from debate.md (line 24) was: 'maintain only the thin differential (r.ts S4 dispatch + workflow.ts + trust layer).' Phase 0 inverts this by adding 20 synthesizers that have nothing to do with bio/polyglot/workflow — the fork's only justified niche. If OW becomes a superset of CG's full general dispatch graph, it must track ALL of CG's future synthesizer changes, not just the bio-specific ones. This is not a no-new-evidence reopen — it is a scope argument — but it IS a hidden fork-maintenance tax increase that the strategy presents as free.

ATTACK 2 — The core moat claim (unified structural+lexical = general codegraph replacement) does not survive the correctness-ties finding. The strategy's decisive_experiment gate says 'OW must WIN the content questions (Q7-class) decisively vs both CG and grep-on-cold-index.' But the benchmark (RESULTS.md Part C1) shows correctness tied on all 6 question types across 66 runs. The relevant question is: does winning Q7 (find files containing string X) actually CHANGE OUTCOMES or just reduce tool-call count? The raison-d'etre debate (line 15) already skewered this: 'an agent that gets the right answer via grep in 36 turns versus OmniWeave's 16 turns has STILL gotten the right answer.' If correctness already ties on structural queries AND grep already correctly answers Q7 via Bash, a unified surface saves tool-call count but does not change the outcome. The strategy's honest_risk section acknowledges this for large repos, but the problem is general: on repos <50k files where ripgrep takes <0.5s, the agent's total runtime saving from collapsing two tool calls into one is measured in fractions of a second, not in correctness. The strategy must show that agents FAIL Q7 when forced to use structural-only OW, not just that they use more tool calls.

ATTACK 3 — Phase 2 (FTS5 trigram content index) is a real technical idea but breaks the zero-config promise at meaningful scale AND introduces a new correctness failure mode. The research confirms: 3.3x storage expansion on files, ~3-4GB for Chromium-class repos. But even for OW's typical 5k-50k file repos, the expansion is 500MB-1GB of ADDITIONAL SQLite storage, inside the same WAL file that holds the structural graph. The single-writer WAL cannot parallelize index builds. More critically: FTS5 trigram search is a BM25/token approach — it does NOT answer arbitrary regex queries (the Q7 benchmark used 'contains string X'). If an agent asks 'find all files matching pattern \bfoo.*bar\b', SQLite FTS5 trigram finds candidates (trigrams that could match) then falls back to LIKE or re-read verification. For a complex regex, the 'trigram candidate set' may be empty or trivially large, giving WORSE performance than ripgrep's SIMD-parallel scan. The strategy conflates 'substring search' (where FTS5 trigram excels) with 'regex search' (where it does not). The decisive experiment must run with regex patterns, not just literal strings, or the moat claim is untested.

ATTACK 4 — Phase 1 (UserPromptSubmit prompt hook) is Claude Code-specific and has already been indirectly PARK'd. The CHECKPOINT and PARK table record that CG's own evaluation of 'trace-first steering ported to server-instructions + tool descriptions regressed wall-clock vs baseline' (CLAUDE.md CHECKPOINT references). The CodeCompass finding (58% zero adoption) is about natural-mode agent behavior — and the paper showed that a checklist-at-END prompt formatting ALONE boosted adoption to 100% without any hook mechanism. OW already has CLAUDE.md / AGENTS.md twin agent instructions that function identically to a hook for Claude Code users. Building a UserPromptSubmit hook is only additive for Claude Code ≥ settings.json hook support — it adds zero value for Cursor, Copilot, or any other host agent. The strategy presents this as a general adoption fix when it is a Claude Code-specific integration that may not even be needed given that OW's AGENTS.md already pre-injects structural context into the agent's instruction set.

ATTACK 5 — flashgrep is legally unadoptable and the strategy's alternative (SQLite FTS5 trigram) is architecturally WEAKER than what the strategy claims it beats. flashgrep is a private repository (GitHub 404 confirmed). There is NO public license. OW cannot redistribute, wrap, or bundle it. The strategy correctly notes this under honest_risk but then proceeds to use flashgrep's 36x speedup claim as motivation for the entire Phase 2. The honest comparison is: OW with SQLite FTS5 trigram vs cold ripgrep. For repos under 10k files, ripgrep's cold-scan latency is <100ms. FTS5 trigram insertion during indexAll serializes through SQLite's single writer — at 10k files x 5KB average, that is 50MB of content ingested into an FTS5 virtual table while also building the structural graph. The build time overhead is not benchmarked. If the index build adds 30+ seconds to `omniweave init` on a medium repo, that breaks OW's 'zero-config, just works' positioning.

ATTACK 6 — The strategy's DEFAULT_MCP_TOOLS reduction from 5 to 1 (Phase 3) directly contradicts OW's existing eval evidence without citing a new number. The strategy says 'absorbing node/search/callers/impact functionality into explore's routing' would reduce to 1 tool to match CG's validated minimal-surface design. But CG's minimal-surface design is already validated FROM CG's OWN A/B — NOT from OW's A/B. OW's rounds 1-7 showed the 5-tool surface working alongside the full eval suite passing 1764 tests. Collapsing to 1 tool means that any agent that explicitly calls omniweave_node or omniweave_search must now route through explore — changing the tool schema invalidates every agent session's KV-cached tool definition prefix on the first call, causing a cache miss on every existing OW-integrated agent. This is a breaking change to the MCP API surface that would require version bumping AND re-establishing all existing agent configurations. The ROI of this change must be proven with an OW-specific A/B showing fewer total calls, not borrowed from CG's different-tool-surface design.

ATTACK 7 — The 'honest probability' of making OW a GENERAL codegraph replacement is structurally constrained by the market crowding finding. The ecosystem scan found: codegraph (47k stars, active), codebase-memory-mcp (C binary, 158 languages, arXiv paper), Serena (25k stars, LSP), blarify (SCIP), code-graph-mcp (ast-grep). Every tool claims 'fewer tokens + fewer tool calls.' OW has 0 published star-count, 0 arXiv paper, 0 press coverage. The FTS5 trigram content index idea (Phase 2) would need to be built, validated, shipped, AND discovered by the community WHILE codegraph actively ships (CHANGELOG shows React Native Expo bridges, GoFrame routes, WSL2 watcher fixes, massive-repo SQL limits — all in recent versions). If OW is 3 months building FTS5 trigram infrastructure and codegraph ships a simpler ripgrep-subprocess integration in its next release, the 'general moat' disappears before it ships. The competitive risk is not that OW's idea is wrong — it is that OW has no distribution advantage and a 1-person maintenance team vs an upstream with 47k stars.

ATTACK 8 — Phase 0's claim that syncing CG's synthesizers makes 'OW at least as good as CG on every common TS/JS/Java/Python/Ruby/C repo' is unvalidated and the gap may be smaller than reported. The research finds CG's callback-synthesizer.ts has 13 references to piniaStoreEdges/vuexDispatch/redux/MediatR/celery/sidekiq/Laravel/Spring/fnptr in the snapshot — but the OW benchmark showed correctness TIES across all repos including TS/JS/Python repos that Pinia/Redux/MediatR patterns would appear in. If OW ALREADY TIES CG on correctness despite the missing synthesizers, those synthesizers are either not triggered on the tested repos OR agents reach correct answers via alternative paths. The strategy assumes the synthesizer gap causes measurable correctness degradation for agents — but the 7-round A/B provides no evidence of this. The gap may be a GRAPH QUALITY gap (OW shows fewer edges) without being an AGENT OUTCOME gap (agents still answer correctly by reading files). Building Phase 0 to close a gap that has no demonstrated agent-outcome effect is potentially wasted effort that delays the one genuinely new idea (FTS5 body indexing).
park_violations: Phase 0 ('sync CG's 20 general synthesizers for TS/JS/Java/Python/Ruby/C general repos') expands OW's maintained differential beyond the PARK'd scope. The debate.md synthesis verdict explicitly PARK'd the direction of 'full-stack parallel project with 8633 lines of diverged hot-path code' and recommended maintaining ONLY 'r.ts S4 dispatch + workflow.ts + trust layer.' Syncing Pinia/Vuex/Redux/MediatR/Celery/Sidekiq/Laravel/Spring/C-fnptr synthesizers adds ~625 lines of general-repo synthesizers OUTSIDE the bio/polyglot/workflow niche, making OW's maintained differential 3-4x larger. No new contradicting number is provided — the strategy's justification ('OW is actively weaker on general single-language repos') is not new evidence, it is a RESTATEMENT of the gap that the debate already evaluated and resolved as 'thin differential fork, not full-stack fork.'

Phase 3 ('reduce DEFAULT_MCP_TOOLS from 5 to 1, absorbing node/search/callers/impact into explore') reopens the 'lower-shape-tax NO-GO' PARK direction. The PARK table records 'lower-shape-tax NO-GO (marginal +682 tok)' from round 6. The round 6 finding was that the fixed MCP tool-layer tax of ~34k tokens is a base harness cost, not OW-specific, and OW's marginal contribution was only +682 tokens — there was NO ROI in further reducing the tool surface. Collapsing to 1 tool does not change the base harness cost; it only changes which tool the agent calls. The strategy provides no new A/B number showing 1-tool OW outperforms 5-tool OW on any metric. The CG default-1-tool evidence is CG's own internal design choice validated on CG's surface, not OW's — it is not 'new contradicting evidence' for OW's PARK'd direction.
redteam.is_moat_real: ["The claimed GENERAL moat (unified structural+lexical surface via FTS5 trigram body indexing) is CONDITIONALLY REAL but the strategy overclaims its scope and buildability. What is real: OW currently has NO file-body content search (confirmed from schema.sql — nodes_fts covers only name/qualified_name/docstring/signature); neither does CG (same confirmed schema). Adding FTS5 trigram over raw file bytes would be a categorical new capability that neither CG nor any other structural graph MCP tool currently provides from a single in-process index. This gap is real and general (applies to any repo, any language). What is NOT real: (1) The moat 'beats flashgrep' — flashgrep is a private binary OW cannot redistribute; the comparison is irrelevant. (2) The moat 'beats grep' — SQLite FTS5 trigram beats cold ripgrep ONLY for repos >10k files where ripgrep scan latency exceeds the FTS5 lookup latency; for smaller repos, ripgrep wins on zero-build-cost. (3) The moat makes OW a GENERAL codegraph REPLACEMENT — correctness ties on all 6 structural question types mean the moat is purely in tool-call-count reduction, which the prior PARK found adds +682 tokens marginal benefit at fixed ~34k base. The honest version: adding FTS5 body indexing makes OW STRICTLY A SUPERSET of CG on query coverage (can answer Q7 that CG cannot), but the agent-outcome impact (correctness) is unchanged and the turn-count savings are only measurable on Q7-class queries, which are a minority of structural-graph agent interactions. The moat is real as a capability extension; it is NOT real as a general replacement driver."]
redteam.buildability: ["Phase 2 (FTS5 trigram content index) is the most technically buildable part of the strategy. It requires: (a) adding a content_fts VIRTUAL TABLE USING fts5(path UNINDEXED, content, tokenize='trigram') to schema.sql — confirmed technically feasible in node:sqlite 22.22.3 (live test: CREATE VIRTUAL TABLE t USING fts5(x, tokenize=trigram) works); (b) populating it during indexAll for files <= MAX_FILE_SIZE — additive to the existing extraction pipeline, no WASM worker changes needed; (c) exposing as a second mode in omniweave_search or as a fallback path in explore. Storage overhead of 3.3x confirmed on a real file (tools.ts 220KB → 728KB). Estimated 500MB-1GB for a 20k-file TypeScript repo — borderline acceptable, not zero-config friendly for large repos. Build-time write pressure through SQLite's single-writer WAL is the main risk: inserting full file content alongside structural graph build could add 30-120s to init on medium repos (not benchmarked). The FTS5 trigram approach stays fully in-process with zero new binary dependencies — this is the correct choice over any flashgrep/Zoekt subprocess. Phase 0 (synthesizer sync) is also buildable: bounded copy of 625 lines with eval fixtures, but the ROI is unproven as noted. Phase 1 (UserPromptSubmit hook) is buildable in 1 week but Claude Code-specific with questionable marginal ROI given OW's existing AGENTS.md/CLAUDE.md twin. Phase 3 (tool surface collapse to 1) is buildable but is a BREAKING API CHANGE with a PARK'd direction and insufficient justification. Overall: Phase 2 alone is the right bet; Phases 0, 1, 3 need new evidence before building."]
redteam.honest_probability: ["10-20% probability of making OW a GENERAL codegraph replacement. The structural constraints are: (1) correctness already ties on all structural question types — new capabilities improve coverage (Q7) but not correctness; (2) the market is crowded (codegraph 47k stars, codebase-memory-mcp arXiv paper, Serena 25k stars) with no OW distribution advantage; (3) OW is a 1-person fork of a 47k-star actively-shipped upstream, which means every CG improvement creates re-sync pressure; (4) the one new capability idea (FTS5 body indexing) is real and uncrowded but solves a minority of agent queries (Q7-class), not the majority. The more honest framing: 60-70% probability of becoming a NICHE TOOL that is clearly better than CG for bio/polyglot/workflow repos plus meaningfully better at Q7-class queries for all repos, with a credible evidence base. That is a defensible product — it just is not 'general codegraph replacement.' The ambition to replace CG for ALL users requires winning on structural queries (currently tied) or offering a dramatically better developer experience (distribution, zero-config, faster indexing) — none of which are in the strategy's build plan."]
redteam.final_recommendation: ["DO NOT build the strategy as written. It mixes two good ideas with two PARK violations and one unbuildable claim. Specific verdict by phase: PHASE 0 (synthesizer sync for general repos) — DO NOT BUILD as framed. Syncing 20 general-repo CG synthesizers expands OW's maintained differential 3-4x and reopens the full-stack-fork PARK direction without new evidence. If you want parity on Vue/React/Java repos, build eval fixtures FIRST to confirm the synthesizer gap actually causes agent-outcome degradation, not just graph-quality degradation. If the agent reaches correct answers anyway, the work has zero ROI. PHASE 1 (UserPromptSubmit hook) — LOW PRIORITY, Claude Code-specific. OW's existing AGENTS.md already provides pre-prompt structural injection. Run an A/B comparing natural-mode adoption with AGENTS.md vs without before building a dedicated hook infrastructure. PHASE 2 (FTS5 trigram content index) — BUILD THIS, but ONLY this, as a standalone decision. It is technically feasible, zero new binary dependencies, general (any repo, any language), and fills the one genuine categorical gap vs CG (Q7 / file content search). Gate it behind storage-overhead measurement at 5k/20k/50k file tiers before merging. Expose as a pattern: param in omniweave_search, NOT as a new tool, NOT as a DEFAULT_MCP_TOOLS change. Document honest caveats: trigram search excels at literal substring queries, degrades on complex regex, adds 3-4x storage overhead on file content, and has not been validated as an agent-outcome improvement vs simply running ripgrep in Bash. PHASE 3 (collapse to 1 MCP tool) — DO NOT BUILD. Reopens the shape-tax PARK direction without new OW-specific A/B evidence. CG's 1-tool design is their design; OW has 7 rounds of eval on its 5-tool surface. Changing this is a breaking API change with undefined ROI. PHASE 4 (decisive A/B) — RUN THIS BEFORE BUILDING PHASE 2, not after. The experiment design is sound (5 repos, 10 questions, 3 arms, fail-closed). Run it with JUST the structural graph on Q7-class questions to confirm agents actually need the content index before building it. If agents successfully answer Q7 via Bash+grep in 2-3 tool calls on the 5k-file repo, the FTS5 content index adds token savings but no correctness delta — then the moat is real but thin. If agents fail or regress on Q7 with structural-only OW, the content index has genuine correctness value and Phase 2 is strongly justified. Honest bottom line: OW's best general play is FTS5 body indexing (Phase 2) gated by a decisive A/B (Phase 4 first), maintaining ONLY the bio/polyglot/workflow differential (S4 dispatch + workflow DAG + trust layer), and resisting every temptation to expand the fork surface to match CG's full general-repo synthesizer coverage. The ambition to replace CG for ALL users is ~10-20% achievable; the ambition to be clearly the best tool for bio/polyglot/workflow repos PLUS the only structural graph with indexed content search is ~60-70% achievable and worth building toward."]
